title: Goto statements
summary_md:
## Goto Statements

`goto` is one of C++'s oldest control-flow tools — it unconditionally jumps execution to a labelled point anywhere in the same function. The syntax is straightforward:

```cpp
#include <iostream>
int main() {
    int x { 0 };
try_again:
    std::cin >> x;
    if (x <= 0)
        goto try_again;   // jump back to label
    std::cout << "Got: " << x << '\n';
}
```

A **label** is an identifier followed by a colon (`try_again:` above). `goto` then names that label as its target. Both must live in the same function — you cannot jump between functions.

### Why goto almost never belongs in modern C++

Every use-case for `goto` is handled more clearly by structured alternatives:

| What you might reach for `goto` | Use instead |
|---|---|
| Retry a read (as above) | `while` or `do-while` loop (lesson 8.8/8.9) |
| Break out of nested loops | Refactor into a function, or use a flag variable |
| Jump to cleanup on error | RAII (destructors run automatically) |
| State machine transitions | `switch` + enum (see lesson 8-5 and 8-6) |

Python has no `goto` at all — the language designers considered it harmful enough to omit entirely. C++ keeps it for legacy compatibility, but the [C++ Core Guidelines](https://isocpp.github.io/CppCoreGuidelines/) say: *"Don't use goto."*

The core problem is **spaghetti control flow**: once `goto` is present, you can no longer read a function top-to-bottom and understand what runs when. Debuggers, optimizers, and the next developer (including future you) all struggle.

There is one narrow exception you may see in generated or very low-level C code: a `goto cleanup` pattern when C-style resource management demands a single exit point. In C++ this is replaced entirely by RAII — objects clean up in destructors automatically, so the pattern is unnecessary.

### The C++20 picture

`constexpr if` (lesson 8-4), `switch` with scoped enums (lessons 8-5/8-6), and structured exception handling all make `goto` even less necessary. There is no C++20 feature that rehabilitates `goto`. Recognise it when you read legacy code; never reach for it in new code.

exercises.title: Identify and fix goto-based control flow
exercises.prompt_md:
The starter code uses `goto` to skip over a block of output. Your task is to **replace the goto and its label with an equivalent `if-else` statement** so the logic is identical but the control flow is structured.

The program reads two integers `a` and `b` from stdin.
- If `a > b`, it prints `a is greater`.
- Otherwise it prints `b is greater or equal`.
- Either way it then prints `Done.`

Do **not** change what the program prints — only restructure the control flow.

test_cases.label: b greater than a
test_cases.expected_stdout:
b is greater or equal
Done.

test_cases.label: a equals b
test_cases.expected_stdout:
b is greater or equal
Done.

exercises.title: Replace goto with if-else chain
exercises.prompt_md:
## Replace goto with if-else chain

The starter code uses `goto` to implement a multi-way branch based on a menu choice. Your task is to **replace all gotos and labels with an equivalent `if/else if/else` chain**.

The program reads a single integer `choice` from stdin:
- If `choice` is `1`, print `Action: Save`
- If `choice` is `2`, print `Action: Load`
- If `choice` is `3`, print `Action: Quit`
- For any other value, print `Action: Unknown`

After the action line, always print `Menu complete.`

Do **not** change what the program prints — only restructure the control flow to eliminate `goto`.

```
Sample input:  2
Sample output:
Action: Load
Menu complete.
```
